options.label: A
options.text: The function f defined by f(x) = { 22, for x < 1; 3x + 2, for x \geq 1 } is neither differentiable nor continuous at x=1.

options.label: B
options.text: The function f(x) = x^2 |x| is twice differentiable at x=0.

options.label: C
options.text: If f is continuous at x=5 and f(5)=2, then \lim_{x \to 5} f(x) = 2 exists.

options.label: D
options.text: If \lim_{x \to a} f(x) + g(x) exists and \lim_{x \to a} f(x) - g(x) exists, then \lim_{x \to a} f(x) g(x) need not exist.

options_latex.A: The function f defined by f(x) = \begin{cases} 22, & \text{for } x < 1; \\ 3x + 2, & \text{for } x \geq 1 \end{cases} is neither differentiable nor continuous at x=1.
options_latex.B: The function f(x) = x^2 |x| is twice differentiable at x=0.
options_latex.C: If f is continuous at x=5 and f(5)=2, then \lim_{x \to 5} f(x) = 2 exists.
options_latex.D: If \lim_{x \to a} (f(x) + g(x)) exists and \lim_{x \to a} (f(x) - g(x)) exists, then \lim_{x \to a} (f(x) g(x)) need not exist.

question_text: Which of the following statements is/are correct?
question_text_latex: Which of the following statements is/are correct?

options.label: A
options.text: There exists a function f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R} which is discontinuous at every point in [0,1] and |f(x)| is continuous at every point in [0,1]

options.label: B
options.text: Let F(x) = f(x) \cdot g(x). If f(x) is differentiable at x = a, f(a) = 0 and g(x) is continuous at x = a, then F(x) is always differentiable at x = a.

options.label: C
options.text: If f'(R) a = 2 and f'(L) a = 3, then f(x) is non-differentiable at x = a but will be always continuous at x = a.

options.label: D
options.text: If f(a) and f(b) possess opposite signs, then there must exist at least one solution of the equation f(x) = 0 in (a,b) provided f is continuous on [a,b].

options_latex.A: There exists a function f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R} \text{ which is discontinuous at every point in } [0,1] \text{ and } |f(x)| \text{ is continuous at every point in } [0,1]
options_latex.B: Let F(x) = f(x) \cdot g(x). \text{ If } f(x) \text{ is differentiable at } x = a, f(a) = 0 \text{ and } g(x) \text{ is continuous at } x = a, \text{ then } F(x) \text{ is always differentiable at } x = a.
options_latex.C: If f'(R) a = 2 \text{ and } f'(L) a = 3, \text{ then } f(x) \text{ is non-differentiable at } x = a \text{ but will be always continuous at } x = a.
options_latex.D: If f(a) \text{ and } f(b) \text{ possess opposite signs, then there must exist at least one solution of the equation } f(x) = 0 \text{ in } (a,b) \text{ provided } f \text{ is continuous on } [a,b].
